_meta.doc: Species catalog. v0.2.0 introduces the four-species saproxylic core for the Białowieża beech-log scenario. Citations are short-form; full bibliography belongs in research/ (TBD).

evidence_level_ladder.locality_observed: Documented in a peer-reviewed paper from the specific scenario locality
evidence_level_ladder.regional_typical: Documented in the broader region's fauna list (e.g., European saproxylic for Białowieża)
evidence_level_ladder.genus_documented: Genus is well-documented in this guild/niche; species inferred
evidence_level_ladder.inferred_from_guild: No species-level documentation in this niche; presence inferred from guild ecology

_meta._audit_note_v0_2_0: All four species entered at 'regional_typical' — they appear in European saproxylic fauna lists (Speight 1989, Stokland et al. 2012) but I have not yet verified each against the published Białowieża inventory. Promote to 'locality_observed' after that audit.

beauveria_bassiana.biology_notes: Cordycipitaceae. Cosmopolitan entomopathogenic fungus — the white-muscardine epidemic of silkworms (Agostino Bassi 1835) is the species' original notoriety. Hyphae penetrate the host cuticle of weakened or recently-dead arthropods and consume the body from inside, then erupt as a fluffy white sporulating mat over the corpse. Hosts include nearly every terrestrial arthropod order; specificity is set by environmental factors rather than fungal preference. In this simulator, modeled as a post-mortem sessile colonizer that nucleates where bug_corpse_g has accumulated.

trametes_versicolor.biology_notes: White-rot polypore. Establishes on partly-decayed sapwood with moisture > 0.4. Decomposes lignin + cellulose, releases fungal biomass (hyphae + spore-bearing pore tissue) that fuels fungivores. Mature bracket forms over ~30 days in optimal conditions; thalli persist for years.

ceratophysella_denticulata.biology_notes: Hypogastruridae. Consumes fungal hyphae and spores in moist-rotted wood. Asexual / parthenogenetic reproduction common. Eggs hatch in 7–10 days in temperate woodland; v0.3.0 uses 7 days as the egg duration.

glomeris_marginata.biology_notes: Glomeridae. Consumes partly-decayed wood + leaf litter; requires wood_decay_stage > 0.3 to access lignin breakdown products. Pill-rolling defense against most invertebrate predators but not centipedes the size of L. forficatus. Eggs ~30 days to hatch. Real adult-to-adult breeding cycle is 1+ year (Hopkin & Read 1992); v0.5.0 uses 70-day cooldown as a compromise that keeps populations dynamic over the 120-day scenario.

oniscus_asellus.biology_notes: Oniscidae. Larger and more cosmopolitan than Glomeris; tolerates drier substrates and broader decay-stage range (wood_decay_stage >= 0.2). Slower predator-escape than Glomeris but lacks the conglobation defense, so Lithobius takes them readily. Marsupial brood pouch; eggs incubate in the female until hatched.

neobisium_muscorum.biology_notes: Neobisiidae. Small soft-body predator with pincer-like pedipalps. Hunts springtails, mites, and small soft prey in moist litter and rotting wood crevices. Cannot tackle prey larger than ~6mm body length; doesn't compete directly with Lithobius which targets bigger prey. Has a venomous gland in the pedipalp tip.

sarracenia_purpurea.biology_notes: Sarraceniaceae. The host plant of the phytotelma niche. Captures insects with a waxy interior + downward-pointing hairs; drowned prey accumulates in the trap fluid and is decomposed by bacteria + commensal larvae. The plant absorbs the nitrogen + phosphorus that mineralizes from prey. Doesn't actively consume anything in the simulation — it's the niche scaffold that prey trapping (modeled as a daily input) flows through.

citations: Buckley Hannah Tarter 2003 Annals Ent Soc Am
wyeomyia_smithii.biology_notes: Culicidae. Obligate inhabitant of Sarracenia pitchers throughout its range. Larvae filter-feed on bacteria + protozoa in the water column. Pupates and emerges as a flying adult after ~3 weeks at 18°C; in the simulator, emergence is modeled as old-age 'death' (the adult leaves the niche). Larva is confined to water_column substrate.

metriocnemus_knabi.biology_notes: Chironomidae. Common commensal of Sarracenia + other pitcher-plant species. Larvae live in the detritus + benthos at the pitcher floor, fragmenting prey carcasses + grazing the bacterial film. Their fragmentation of prey accelerates bacterial decomposition — they make food available for Wyeomyia. Pupates in late season + emerges as a midge; modeled here as old-age death.

habrotrocha_rosa.biology_notes: Bdelloidea. Sessile microscopic filter feeder common in Sarracenia pitchers. Modeled as a sessile colony (similar mechanic to Trametes) that processes bacterial biomass on the pitcher floor. Population scales by microscopic colony patches; we represent each patch as one 'sessile' entry with size_cm = 0.3.

calliphora_vicina.biology_notes: Calliphoridae. The classic first-responder of European temperate-zone forensic succession. Females lay 200–300 eggs on a fresh carcass within hours of death; the maggot mass consumes soft tissue at a rate that can clear half a small mammal in a week. Aggregates in moving 'maggot masses' that maintain elevated temperature via metabolic heat. Modeled here as the dominant fresh-stage consumer; pupates and emerges (modeled as old-age death) after ~14–18 days at 18°C.

necrodes_littoralis.biology_notes: Silphidae. Large carrion-beetle that arrives in the bloat-to-active-decay stages (~day 5–15). Adults eat soft tissue + actively predate fly larvae, so they dampen the Calliphora wave and accelerate transitions toward later stages. The species' presence is a forensic indicator of post-mortem interval beyond a few days.

dermestes_lardarius.biology_notes: Dermestidae. Late-stage carrion specialist arriving once the carcass has dried — roughly post-mortem day 20+. Larvae and adults bore through skin, hair, and tendon, taking the keratin-rich tissues other carrion species can't process. Strongly synanthropic; in dry remains they may be the last terrestrial consumers before only skeletal material remains.

aphodius_rufipes.biology_notes: Aphodiidae. Surface-dwelling dung specialist common across European pastures. Adults arrive within hours of a fresh dung deposit and feed on dung directly without tunneling. Larvae develop in the substrate. Generation time short — 14 to 21 days under summer field conditions. Fast turnover means a population can ride a single dung pat from fresh to dried-out crust.

geotrupes_stercorarius.biology_notes: Geotrupidae. Large tunneling dung beetle that excavates vertical shafts under fresh dung and provisions them with dung balls for brood. A single Geotrupes can move ten or twenty grams of substrate per day — substantially more per individual than Aphodius, but at lower densities. Their tunneling is the mechanism by which European pastures avoid being covered in old dung.

saprinus_semistriatus.biology_notes: Histeridae. Predatory beetle common on dung + carrion. Eats fly larvae directly — the high-density Muscidae larvae that develop in fresh dung interiors are the main prey. Metallic green-bronze elytra; can play dead (the 'clown' part of the common name) when disturbed. Larvae also predatory.

mantis_religiosa.biology_notes: Mantidae. The classic ambush predator — perches on a stem or flower, head rotating to track movement within forty degrees of forward, raptorial forelegs cocked. Takes prey up to its own body length. Females are sexually cannibalistic in roughly thirty percent of mating encounters; some sub-populations are obligately cannibalistic. Monogenerational in temperate Europe; eggs overwinter in an ootheca attached to a stem near ground level, hatch in early spring.

pieris_brassicae.biology_notes: Pieridae. Common European meadow + garden butterfly. Adults feed on nectar from a wide range of flower species; larvae (caterpillars) feed on Brassicaceae — not modeled here (this scenario tracks adults visiting the meadow). Wingspan 50–65 mm; large white wings with black tips. Slow flier compared to mantis strike speed, which is the species' vulnerability in a Mantis-occupied meadow.

apis_mellifera.biology_notes: Apidae. Workers from a nearby hive forage at flowers, drinking nectar and depositing pollen. Each worker visits hundreds of flowers per day. Modeled as individual workers entering the niche, feeding, and returning to the hive (modeled as old-age departure). Faster than Pieris; one of the species the mantis must work harder to catch. Forages in the warmest hours.

chorthippus_brunneus.biology_notes: Acrididae. The most common grasshopper of European tall-grass meadows. Adults eat leaf tissue of Festuca, Lolium, and similar grasses. Annual generation, overwinters as egg pod buried in soil. Crepuscular leap-flight is its main predator escape; the mantis's strike is too fast to outpace, but the grasshopper has to be detected first.

ips_typographus.biology_notes: Curculionidae: Scolytinae. The major bark beetle of European spruce (Picea abies). Adults aggregate via pheromones to overcome the host tree's defensive resin; once the tree is colonized, the female excavates a horizontal maternal gallery in the inner bark and lays eggs along its sides. Larvae tunnel perpendicular to the gallery, eating phloem as they go. The species vectors Ophiostoma blue-stain fungi which disable the tree's resin defense — without the fungus, spruce trees can drown the beetles in resin; with it, the tree dies in a few weeks. Three generations per summer in warming Alpine climates; outbreaks since 2018 have killed millions of hectares of European spruce.

thanasimus_formicarius.biology_notes: Cleridae. The principal European predator of bark beetles. Adults are ant-mimic in shape and movement — narrow waist, fast running gait, red-and-black warning coloration — and hunt adult bark beetles on the bark surface. Larvae hunt bark beetle larvae inside the galleries. Both stages are pheromone-tracking; Thanasimus eavesdrops on Ips's aggregation pheromone, arriving at exactly the trees Ips just attacked. Cannot overcome outbreak densities alone, but in low-density years can suppress bark beetle populations significantly.

coeloides_bostrichorum.biology_notes: Braconidae. Solitary endoparasitoid of bark beetle larvae. The female wasp locates a host larva through the bark by vibrations + scent (the larva chewing is audible to the wasp's pedipalps), then drills her ovipositor through the bark and into the larva to lay a single egg inside. The host continues developing normally for about a week while the wasp larva grows inside its body cavity, then the wasp emerges, killing the host. Specific to Ips typographus and related Scolytinae in the European spruce zone. One of the bark beetle complex's most important natural enemies after the cold winter.

citations: Linnakoski et al 2012 Annual Review Phytopathology
ophiostoma_bicolor.biology_notes: Ophiostomataceae. Ascomycete fungus vectored by Ips typographus in mycangia (specialized fungal-spore-carrying pockets) on its body. When the beetle introduces it into a tree, the fungus spreads through the phloem and ray parenchyma, disabling the tree's resin defense and producing the characteristic blue-grey staining of the wood. Without Ophiostoma the bark beetle attack often fails; with it, even healthy spruce trees can be killed by a moderate beetle population.

dytiscus_marginalis.biology_notes: Dytiscidae. Adults of the great diving beetle hunt by sight + chemoreception in pond water columns; air-breathers that surface periodically to refill the elytral air store. Larvae are the famous 'water tigers' — elongated with massive forward-curving mandibles that inject paralytic digestive enzymes into prey. Both stages take prey up to small-vertebrate size; in alpine ponds the larva is often the apex predator. Three-year life cycle; pupation on land (modeled as old-age departure here).

aeshna_juncea.biology_notes: Aeshnidae. The larval stage of one of the most common European dragonflies. Sits in pond mud or emergent vegetation, ambushes passing prey by rapid extension of its hinged labium (the lower lip) — a strike too fast for prey reflex. Two-to-four-year larval development depending on altitude; emerges from water by climbing emergent vegetation, dries cuticle, flies as adult (modeled here as old-age departure). One generation per pond cohort.

culex_pipiens.biology_notes: Culicidae. Larvae feed by filtering algae and bacteria from the water column with feathered mouthparts. Hang from the water surface tension via a posterior breathing siphon; submerge to escape predators. Develop in 7-10 days at 18°C, then pupate; pupae also air-breathe at the surface. Adults emerge and disperse — modeled here as old-age departure. Common pond inhabitant + main prey of nearly every aquatic predator at this scale.

daphnia_magna.biology_notes: Daphniidae. The most-studied freshwater zooplankter. Filter-feeds algae + bacteria from the water column with feathered thoracic legs. Modeled here as a sessile-resource colony rather than individual agents — a Daphnia patch represents a swarm of hundreds of individuals at the local cell. Mineralizes algal biomass into the daphnia_density field that fish + larger predators forage on. Cyclical parthenogenesis; in a small pond a single founding female can establish a colony of thousands within weeks.

fucus_serratus.biology_notes: Fucaceae. Brown alga of the mid-intertidal zone, the canonical 'toothed wrack' identifiable by its serrated lateral margins. Holdfast anchors firmly to rock; thallus dichotomously branches up to 60cm in sheltered pools. Photosynthesizes when submerged at high tide + during sunlit low tide; the carbon fixed is the base of the rockpool food web. Provides shelter for limpets, periwinkles, and small crustaceans.

citations: Crisp Southward 1958 J Marine Biol Assoc
semibalanus_balanoides.biology_notes: Balanidae. The defining sessile crustacean of the upper- and mid-intertidal Atlantic. The white volcano-cone test is calcareous and permanent; inside, a modified shrimp-like body extends its cirri — feathered hindlimbs — through the operculum to comb plankton from the water with the tide. Boreal-Arctic species that defines northern rocky-shore biological zonation. Lives roughly five years; competes for substrate with mussels and Chthamalus.

citations: Chomsky et al 2004 J Exp Biol
actinia_equina.biology_notes: Actiniidae. The carmine-red anemone of mid-intertidal rock surfaces and pool sides. Retracts to a closed jellied dome when exposed at low tide; opens into a crown of 192 stinging tentacles when submerged. Beadlet acrorhagi (the blue 'beads' for which the species is named) ringed around the column are deployed in agonistic combat against neighbouring anemones — the species is famously territorial. Eats anything that touches the tentacles small enough to subdue: planktonic copepods, larval fish, small crustaceans.

citations: Jenkins et al 2008 Marine Ecology Progress
patella_vulgata.biology_notes: Patellidae. The conical limpet of European Atlantic rocky shores. Adheres so firmly to rock by muscular foot that pulling one off requires a sudden sideways blow. Grazes biofilm and microalgae from the rock surface with a radula bearing nearly two thousand mineralised iron-and-silica teeth (the strongest known biological material). Each adult occupies a home scar — a slight depression in the rock matching its own shell — and returns to it after each grazing excursion. Modeled here as sessile because the home-scar fidelity makes individuals effectively positional.

carcinus_maenas.biology_notes: Carcinidae. The defining motile predator of European Atlantic rockpools. Five pairs of pereopods, two raptorial chelae that crush mollusc shells and tear into anemones. Walks sideways; tolerates broad salinity ranges (one of the most invasive marine species globally — naturalised on five continents). At low tide hides under fucus or in rock crevices; at high tide hunts across the pool. Takes anything it can subdue, including conspecifics during molting (cannibalism on soft-shell stage).

lithobius_forficatus.biology_notes: Lithobiidae. Active predator hunting smaller arthropods in galleries and surface crevices. Venom mandibles. In v0.2.0 the predation success is deterministic when in-range; future versions add prey-flee + escape rolls. Eggs laid in soil cracks, ~40 days to hatch; sub-adult stadia elided.
